meta.description: Tier 1 Food Ontology — protein food entities. Food Protein Catalog v1 complete (ONTOLOGY-02A).

categories.name: Animal Protein Foods
categories.introduction: Animal protein foods include meat, poultry, game, and seafood used as the primary protein on the plate. These entities anchor wine pairing reasoning through fat, protein, and cooking context.
categories.summary: Meat, poultry, game, and seafood protein foods for wine pairing — organized by biological group and cut.
categories.seo_description: Explore animal protein foods — beef, pork, poultry, seafood, and game — and how each cut pairs with wine through fat, texture, and flavor.

categories.name: Plant Protein Foods
categories.introduction: Plant protein foods include legumes, soy, grains, nuts, and seeds — foundational for vegetarian pairing and flexitarian plates. Botanical metadata replaces mammalian cut concepts.
categories.summary: Legumes, soy foods, grains, nuts and seeds — plant_category with plant_part and edible_structure.
categories.seo_description: Legumes, tofu, seitan, quinoa, almonds, and more — plant protein entities with botanical intrinsic metadata.

categories.name: Fungi Protein Foods
categories.introduction: Fungi protein foods include culinary mushrooms and fermented mycoprotein — umami-rich plant-adjacent proteins. edible_structure marks fungal_body vs processed mycoprotein products.
categories.summary: Mushrooms and mycoprotein — final Food Protein Catalog v1 groups.
categories.seo_description: Button to porcini mushrooms and mycoprotein products — fungi protein with fungal_body and processed edible_structure metadata.

groups.name: Beef
groups.introduction: Beef encompasses cuts from cattle (Bos taurus), from premium steaks to braising roasts. Marbling, collagen, and cooking method drive wine pairing — tannin meets protein and fat on rich cuts; acidity and lighter body suit lean muscles.
groups.summary: Beef cuts from rib, loin, chuck, round, and plate — the foundation of red wine pairing.

faq.q: Why is beef the classic red wine pairing?
faq.a: Beef protein and intramuscular fat bind with tannin in red wine, softening astringency while the wine's structure matches the meat's richness.

faq.q: Do all beef cuts pair with the same wine?
faq.a: No. Ribeye and brisket want bolder, tannic reds; lean round and eye of round suit medium reds or need sauce and marinade to balance high-tannin wines.

groups.seo_description: Beef cuts from ribeye to brisket — learn how marbling and cooking method guide wine pairing for each muscle.

groups.name: Pork
groups.introduction: Pork spans lean loin to rich belly and shoulder — the most versatile everyday meat for global cooking. Cured forms (bacon, pancetta) behave differently from fresh roasts; fat content and salt drive wine pairing more than tannin alone.
groups.summary: Fresh and cured pork cuts from belly, loin, shoulder, and leg.

faq.q: Why does pork pair with both white and red wine?
faq.a: Lean loin and tenderloin suit aromatic whites and light reds; rich shoulder, belly, and BBQ want fruit-forward reds or sparkling with acidity.

faq.q: Is cured pork different for pairing?
faq.a: Yes — bacon, pancetta, and ham bring salt and smoke; high-tannin reds often clash unless the dish balances fat and sweetness.

groups.seo_description: Pork belly to tenderloin — fresh roasts, ribs, and cured charcuterie with intrinsic metadata for wine pairing.

groups.name: Lamb
groups.introduction: Lamb is young sheep (Ovis aries) — milder and more tender than mutton, central to Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and British cooking. Fat content is moderate; herb and spice preparations drive wine pairing as much as cut choice.
groups.summary: Lamb cuts from rack and loin to shoulder, shank, and ground — classic European pairing context.

faq.q: Why is lamb classic with Bordeaux and Rhône reds?
faq.a: Moderate fat and herbal crusts match medium tannin reds; gamey older lamb wants earthier, structured wines.

faq.q: Does lamb always pair with red wine?
faq.a: Lean loin and grilled chops suit aromatic whites and rosé; rich shoulder and shank braises want fuller reds.

groups.seo_description: Rack, loin, leg, shoulder, and shank — lamb cuts with intrinsic metadata for wine pairing across roasting, grilling, and braising.

groups.name: Veal
groups.introduction: Veal is young cattle (Bos taurus) — pale, delicate, and lean compared to beef. Italian and French traditions center on cutlets, osso buco, and braised breast; mild flavor suits elegant whites and lighter reds.
groups.summary: Veal cutlets, chops, loin, shank, and ground — delicate protein for classic European wine pairings.

faq.q: Why does veal pair with white wine more often than beef?
faq.a: Mild flavor and lower fat suit crisp whites and light reds; heavy tannic reds can overpower delicate veal.

faq.q: What is osso buco?
faq.a: Osso buco is braised cross-cut veal shank — marrow-rich, traditionally served with gremolata and Barolo or Barbaresco.

groups.seo_description: Veal cutlets, osso buco shank, loin roasts, and ground veal — intrinsic metadata for wine pairing without graph relationships.

groups.name: Poultry
groups.introduction: Poultry spans chicken, turkey, duck, goose, and quail — everyday to celebratory proteins with wide pairing range. Species and cut (white vs dark meat, fat content) matter more than tannin alone for wine matching.
groups.summary: Chicken, turkey, duck, goose, and quail — species-tagged cuts for filtering, pairing, and future dish connectivity.

faq.q: Why does poultry pair with both white and red wine?
faq.a: Lean breast suits crisp whites and rosé; rich duck, thigh meat, and roast preparations handle light to medium reds.

faq.q: How does species metadata help?
faq.a: The species field filters chicken, turkey, duck, goose, and quail without adding hierarchy depth — enabling analytics and search across a flat entity model.

groups.seo_description: Chicken to quail — whole birds, breasts, thighs, and ground poultry with species and anatomical metadata for wine pairing.

groups.name: Wild Game
groups.introduction: Wild game spans cervids, bison, rabbit, hare, and wild boar — lean, seasonal proteins with distinct pairing logic. Species tagging separates venison, elk, and boar without adding hierarchy depth.
groups.summary: Venison, elk, bison, rabbit, hare, and wild boar — internationally recognized game with species and anatomical metadata.

faq.q: Why is wild game leaner than domestic meat?
faq.a: Wild and pasture-raised game move more and carry less fat — wines need acidity and moderate tannin; avoid drying lean cuts.

faq.q: How does wild boar differ from pork?
faq.a: Wild boar (Sus scrofa) is gamier and leaner than domestic pork (Sus scrofa domesticus) — pairing often follows Italian game traditions with Sangiovese and Nebbiolo.

groups.seo_description: Venison, elk, bison, rabbit, hare, and wild boar — game cuts with species metadata for wine pairing and future dish connectivity.

groups.name: Fin Fish
groups.introduction: Fin fish span fatty salmon and tuna to lean cod and sole — acidity-forward whites and sparkling are classic, while grilled swordfish and tuna suit medium reds. Part of the Seafood domain under Animal Protein Foods.
groups.summary: Globally recognized fin fish — fillets, steaks, and whole fish with species and cut metadata.

faq.q: Why do fatty fish pair with white wine?
faq.a: High acidity in Sauvignon Blanc and Chardonnay cuts richness; oak and body match salmon and lobster-adjacent preparations.

faq.q: How is fin fish different from shellfish for pairing?
faq.a: Fin fish emphasize texture and fat; shellfish add brine and sweetness — descriptor and acid balance shift accordingly.

groups.seo_description: Globally recognized fin fish — fillets, steaks, and whole fish with species and cut metadata.

groups.name: Crustaceans
groups.introduction: Crustaceans — lobster, crab, shrimp, and crawfish — bring sweet protein and brine. Butter, garlic, and smoke drive wine choice toward Chardonnay, Champagne, and off-dry whites.
groups.summary: Lobster, crab, shrimp, prawn, and crawfish — portion and whole cuts with seafood vocabulary.

faq.q: Does lobster always need Champagne?
faq.a: Classic but not mandatory — rich Chardonnay, Albariño, and dry rosé work when butter and citrus lead the plate.

faq.q: How do shrimp and prawn differ in the catalog?
faq.a: Separate species entities with distinct scientific names — prawn typically larger warm-water species.

groups.seo_description: Lobster, crab, shrimp, prawn, and crawfish — portion and whole cuts with seafood vocabulary.

groups.name: Mollusks
groups.introduction: Mollusks — oysters, mussels, clams, and scallops — are briny, tender, and often steamed or raw. Raw oysters want Muscadet and Chablis; seared scallops suit richer whites and light reds.
groups.summary: Bivalves and gastropods — oysters, mussels, clams, scallops, abalone, and conch.

faq.q: Why are oysters classic with Muscadet?
faq.a: High acidity and minerality mirror salinity without overpowering delicate oyster flavor.

faq.q: Are scallops treated differently from clams?
faq.a: Scallops are adductor muscle portions; clams and oysters are whole-shellfish entities — different cut_type metadata.

groups.seo_description: Bivalves and gastropods — oysters, mussels, clams, scallops, abalone, and conch.

groups.name: Cephalopods
groups.introduction: Cephalopods — squid, octopus, and cuttlefish — are firm, ink-rich, and grill-friendly. High heat and olive oil preparations pair with Vermentino, Albariño, and southern Italian whites.
groups.summary: Squid, octopus, cuttlefish — tubes, tentacles, and prepared calamari with seafood vocabulary.

faq.q: Is calamari a separate species?
faq.a: Calamari rings are a prepared product from squid — same species, distinct processing_state and cut metadata.

faq.q: Why tentacles as separate entities?
faq.a: Tentacles are commercially sold and cooked differently from whole cephalopods — distinct pairing context.

groups.seo_description: Squid, octopus, cuttlefish — tubes, tentacles, and prepared calamari with seafood vocabulary.

groups.name: Legumes
groups.introduction: Legumes are dried and cooked seeds — chickpeas, lentils, and beans anchor vegetarian protein worldwide. Spice, acid, and smoke drive wine pairing more than tannin.
groups.summary: Chickpeas, lentils, and beans — botanical legume protein with plant_part and edible_structure metadata.

faq.q: Why are plant foods not organized by cuts?
faq.a: Botanical structure uses plant_part and edible_structure — not steak or roast primals.

faq.q: How does bone_state apply to plants?
faq.a: not_applicable — bone metadata is reserved for animal protein foods.

groups.seo_description: Chickpeas, lentils, and beans — botanical legume protein with plant_part and edible_structure metadata.

groups.name: Mushrooms
groups.introduction: Culinary mushrooms are protein-rich fungi used as center-of-plate or supporting protein — umami drivers for wine pairing through butter, cream, and roast techniques.
groups.summary: Twelve globally recognized edible mushrooms from Agaricus to chanterelle.

faq.q: Why are button, cremini, and portobello separate entities?
faq.a: Same species (Agaricus bisporus) but distinct commercial maturity stages with different cooking and pairing context.

faq.q: How does edible_structure differ from plant_part?
faq.a: Mushrooms use fungal_body; plant_part is empty — fungi are not botanical plants.

groups.seo_description: Twelve canonical edible mushrooms with scientific names and fungal_body edible_structure.

groups.name: Mycoprotein
groups.introduction: Mycoprotein is fermented fungal protein sold as formed fillets, pieces, and grounds — generic product classes, not trademarked brand names.
groups.summary: Five mycoprotein product forms — fillet, pieces, grounds, steak, and generic base.

faq.q: Why no brand names in the catalog?
faq.a: Ontology entities are canonical food products; trademarks belong in editorial content, not permanent graph IDs.

faq.q: What organism produces mycoprotein?
faq.a: Fusarium venenatum — fermented into high-protein food matrix.

groups.seo_description: Generic mycoprotein product classes with processed edible_structure — no trademarked brand entities.

protein_foods.name: Ribeye Steak
protein_foods.summary: Ribeye is a premium beef cut from the rib section with abundant marbling and rich flavor. High fat and protein make it a classic match for full-bodied red wines with firm tannin, which bind with lipids and soften the wine's structure.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Ribeye comes from the upper rib — look for even white marbling (fat streaks) through red muscle. It is one of the richest, most forgiving steak cuts for bold red wine pairings.

faq.q: What is Ribeye Steak?
faq.a: Ribeye comes from the upper rib — look for even white marbling (fat streaks) through red muscle.

faq.q: How should Ribeye Steak be cooked for best results?
faq.a: Rich cuts tolerate medium to medium-rare; render fat slowly on roasts and braises. Rest before serving so juices redistribute.

protein_foods.name: Striploin Steak
protein_foods.summary: Striploin (New York strip) sits between ribeye and tenderloin in richness — moderate marbling with a firmer chew than ribeye. It pairs well with structured reds that have tannin to meet the cut's protein without overpowering its beefy character.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Striploin is the same cut many menus call "New York strip." It has a strip of fat along one edge and less internal marbling than ribeye, but more flavor than lean round cuts.

faq.q: What is Striploin Steak?
faq.a: Striploin is the same cut many menus call "New York strip.

faq.q: How should Striploin Steak be cooked for best results?
faq.a: Cook to medium-rare or medium depending on thickness; rest 5–10 minutes and slice against the grain when the muscle grain is visible.

protein_foods.name: Beef Tenderloin
protein_foods.summary: Tenderloin is the most tender beef muscle with minimal fat and a mild, clean beef flavor. Its leanness suits medium-bodied reds with fine tannin or richer whites with oak structure when served with butter or cream sauces.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Tenderloin runs along the spine — the narrow end is often sold as filet mignon. It is tender but not strongly flavored; sauce and cooking method carry much of the pairing weight.

faq.q: What is Beef Tenderloin?
faq.a: Tenderloin runs along the spine — the narrow end is often sold as filet mignon.

faq.q: How should Beef Tenderloin be cooked for best results?
faq.a: Lean cuts benefit from medium-rare to medium doneness, resting before slice, and slicing against the grain — avoid overcooking which dries the meat and makes tannic wines taste harsh.

protein_foods.name: T-Bone Steak
protein_foods.summary: T-bone combines strip steak and tenderloin separated by a T-shaped bone, offering two textures in one cut. The strip side brings marbling and beef intensity; the tenderloin side stays lean and mild — versatile for structured reds.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: A T-bone is two steaks in one: striploin on one side of the bone, tenderloin on the other. Porterhouse uses the same idea but from a larger section with a bigger tenderloin portion.

faq.q: What is T-Bone Steak?
faq.a: A T-bone is two steaks in one: striploin on one side of the bone, tenderloin on the other.

faq.q: How should T-Bone Steak be cooked for best results?
faq.a: Rich cuts tolerate medium to medium-rare; render fat slowly on roasts and braises. Rest before serving so juices redistribute.

protein_foods.name: Porterhouse Steak
protein_foods.summary: Porterhouse is a large T-bone cut from the rear short loin with a generous tenderloin section and a well-marbled strip. Its size and dual texture support full-bodied reds and longer cellar-worthy bottles for special-occasion roasts.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Porterhouse looks like a T-bone but must include a tenderloin filet at least 1.25 inches across (USDA definition). It is typically thicker and meant for sharing.

protein_foods.name: Prime Rib
protein_foods.summary: Prime rib is a bone-in rib roast cut from the same primal as ribeye, roasted rather than grilled. Slow roasting renders fat and concentrates savory beef flavor — a natural partner for bold, tannic reds and classic roast-beef service.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Prime rib is a roast, not a single steak — usually several rib bones with a cap of fat on top. "Prime" here refers to the cut style, not always USDA Prime grade.

faq.q: What is Prime Rib?
faq.a: Prime rib is a roast, not a single steak — usually several rib bones with a cap of fat on top.

faq.q: How should Prime Rib be cooked for best results?

protein_foods.name: Top Sirloin Steak
protein_foods.summary: Top sirloin is a versatile, moderately marbled steak from the hip area with honest beef flavor and manageable fat. It bridges everyday grilling and structured red wine pairings without the richness demand of ribeye.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Top sirloin is different from bottom sirloin or tri-tip — it is a straightforward grill steak with good flavor and moderate tenderness when not overcooked.

faq.q: What is Top Sirloin Steak?
faq.a: Top sirloin is different from bottom sirloin or tri-tip — it is a straightforward grill steak with good flavor and moderate tenderness when not overcooked.

faq.q: How should Top Sirloin Steak be cooked for best results?
faq.a: Cook to medium-rare or medium depending on thickness; rest 5–10 minutes and slice against the grain when the muscle grain is visible.

protein_foods.name: Tri-Tip
protein_foods.summary: Tri-tip is a triangular bottom-sirloin roast popular on the US West Coast, often Santa Maria–style grilled over oak. Moderate fat and pronounced beefiness pair with Syrah, Zinfandel, and other fruit-forward medium-to-full reds.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Tri-tip is one muscle cut into a roast or steaks. Slice against the grain — the grain changes direction in this triangular cut, which trips up many home cooks.

protein_foods.name: Beef Brisket
protein_foods.summary: Brisket is a tough, collagen-rich chest cut that becomes tender and unctuous after long slow cooking. Smoked or braised brisket's fat, smoke, and umami call for reds with depth — often Syrah, Zinfandel, or softer tannin after long cook times.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Brisket has two layers: the leaner flat and the fattier point (deckle). BBQ brisket is almost always slow-smoked; braised brisket appears in Jewish and Central European traditions.

protein_foods.name: Chuck Roast
protein_foods.summary: Chuck roast comes from the shoulder with good marbling and connective tissue that breaks down in braising or slow roasting. Its savory, gelatin-rich juices suit medium-bodied reds and rustic styles with enough acid to cut richness.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Chuck is the workhorse braising cut — affordable, flavorful, and best cooked low and slow until fork-tender. Pot roast is usually chuck.

protein_foods.name: Beef Short Ribs
protein_foods.summary: Short ribs are cut from the plate or chuck with layers of meat, fat, and bone. Braised or slow-smoked short ribs deliver intense beef flavor and gelatin — pairing territory for full-bodied reds and wines with earthy, savory notes.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Short ribs can be bone-in English cut (thick) or cross-cut flanken (Korean style). Both need long cooking to render fat and soften connective tissue.

faq.q: What is Beef Short Ribs?
faq.a: Short ribs can be bone-in English cut (thick) or cross-cut flanken (Korean style).

faq.q: How should Beef Short Ribs be cooked for best results?

protein_foods.name: Beef Shank
protein_foods.summary: Beef shank is a lean, sinewy leg cut built for braising — osso buco and soup bones share this muscle. Collagen melts into silky broth; pair with medium reds or robust whites when the dish includes tomato, herbs, or reduced wine sauces.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Shank is cross-cut with a marrow bone in the center. It is never a quick-cook steak — think stews, braises, and bone marrow richness.

faq.q: What is Beef Shank?

protein_foods.name: Eye of Round
protein_foods.summary: Eye of round is a very lean roast from the rear leg with minimal marbling and a mild flavor. Without added fat in cooking, it favors lighter or medium-bodied reds and wines with bright acidity rather than aggressive tannin.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Eye of round is one of the leanest beef roasts — easy to dry out. Slice thin, do not overcook, and consider marinades or sauce to add richness for wine pairing.

protein_foods.name: Top Round
protein_foods.summary: Top round is a lean leg cut often roasted or sliced thin for sandwiches. Its low fat content means tannic reds can taste harsh unless the meat is served with sauce, marinade, or moderate doneness with resting.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Top round is sometimes labeled London broil in US markets, though that name applies to several lean cuts. Best served medium-rare and sliced against the grain.

protein_foods.name: Bottom Round

protein_foods.beginner_notes: Bottom round sits below top round on the leg — good value for roasting but benefits from slow cooking or thin slicing. Rump roast is often this cut.

protein_foods.name: Flank Steak
protein_foods.summary: Flank steak is a long, flat abdominal muscle with intense beef flavor and visible grain. Lean but fibrous — must be sliced thin against the grain — it works with medium reds, Malbec, and wines that handle marinades and char.
protein_foods.beginner_notes: Flank is not tender but packs flavor. Always slice against the grain at a sharp angle. Common in fajitas and Asian-style grilled beef.

faq.q: What is Flank Steak?
